data.title: Idacorp: Cash In On The AI Boom And Buy Now
data.description:
As an analyst and an investor, I tend to think of myself as a big-picture guy. Think about the present and extrapolate that to the future. From there, I believe that I can find appealing investment opportunities by taking a more company-specific focus.

When I look at the present and out five years or even 10 years, I only see technology becoming more important in our everyday lives. One key element of the ongoing technological revolution is the expansion of existing data centers and the build-out of new ones. One catalyst is the increased adoption of cloud computing, which Amazon's (AMZN) CEO Andy Jassy believes is in the early stages, with about 85% of global IT spending still being on-premises. Other tailwinds include the growing prevalence of the Internet of Things, generative AI, and autonomous vehicles.

As a result, a forecast from Statista expects the global data center industry to grow by 8.4% annually from $452.5 billion in 2025 revenue to $624.1 billion by 2029.

An indirect way to play this megatrend is electric utilities. Why electric utilities?

Data centers require power to operate. A lot of power. More data centers and bigger data centers are going to require much more power. The share of data centers to total energy consumption is predicted to more than double from 5% in 2025 (224 terawatt hours) to 12% by 2030 (606 TWh).

One of the big beneficiaries of this ongoing technological shift is IDACORP, Inc. (NYSE:IDA). When I last covered IDA with a buy rating in February, I liked that it had access to economical and dependable power via hydroelectricity. Its economically vibrant service territories were another plus. As was its investment-grade balance sheet. The discounted valuation put the buy case over the top.

Today, I'm maintaining my buy rating. IDA plans to invest heavily in its infrastructure to meet quickly growing demand. The company's financial health is respectable. IDA's shares also look to remain undervalued.

Growth Ahead Can Be Powered By Electric Load Expansion

On May 1st, IDA shared its first-quarter earnings report for the period ended March 31st, 2025. The company's total operating revenue declined by 3.7% over the year-ago period to $432.5 million in the quarter. That was $22 million greater than Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter.

What led to this decrease in the top line for the first quarter?

It had to do with IDA's status as a regulated utility. Less volatile wholesale natural gas and power market prices in the western United States resulted in decreased net power supply expenses for Idaho Power (-33.8% for purchased power and -24.8% for fuel expense) in the quarter. By law, IDA is required to pass these savings onto customers as a regulated utility.

The electric utility's retail revenue was partially lifted by the January 1 rate case increase and a 2.6% year-over-year growth rate in the customer count during the first quarter.

IDA's diluted EPS jumped 15.8% over the year-ago period to $1.10 for the first quarter. This was $0.06 above Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter. Lower net power supply expenses helped the company's net profit margin expand by nearly 310 basis points to 13.8% during the quarter. That's how diluted EPS grew at a double-digit clip as operating revenue slightly fell for the quarter.

Looking ahead, IDA has the necessary elements for continued growth. Even with U.S. GDP estimates coming down over the last several months, Moody's GDP growth projections for Idaho for 2025 and 2026 remain unchanged at 4.5% and 3.7%, respectively.

This makes sense as Idaho is a business-friendly state, making it an appealing place to do business for data centers. In response to a question during the Q1 2025 Earnings Call, CEO Lisa Grow confirmed that IDA was still in ongoing negotiations to bring another data center to its service territory.

This tremendous economic outlook should keep up net migration trends into Idaho for many more years. Of course, more net migration into Idaho would be a win for IDA via greater demand for its electricity. That's why the company anticipates that its retail sales will grow by 8.3% annually over the next five years.

Meeting this rising demand is going to require substantial investment on the part of IDA. As I noted in my previous article, the company intends to spend $5.6 billion on capex from 2025 through 2029 to fulfill future demand - - double the spending over the previous five years.

IDA looks to have the financial capacity to execute this capital spending plan, too. As of February 20th, 2025, the utility had no maturities in 2025 and only $116 million in maturities due through 2029 (all of which was due in 2026). In 2030, only another $80 million was due. IDA accordingly possesses a BBB credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

Thanks to the electric utility's growth tailwinds, I'm inclined to agree with the FAST Graphs analyst consensus over the next few years. In 2025, diluted EPS is expected to grow by 6.2% to $5.84. For 2026, it's forecasted that diluted EPS will jump 8.7% to $6.35. In 2027, an additional 8.7% rise in diluted EPS to $6.90 is the current consensus (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to IDA's Q1 2025 Earnings Press Release, IDA's Q1 2025 10-Q Filing, IDA's Q1 2025 Investor Presentation, and IDA's June 2025 Investor Presentation).

Moderate Upside To Fair Value

Since my last article, IDA's shares have been flat as the S&P 500 index (SP500) has edged 1% higher. Nearly four months later, I believe shares are about as appealing.

IDA is priced at a current-year P/E ratio of 19.8. The forward 12-month P/E ratio is even lower at 19. For more context, these valuations are less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 20.5 per FAST Graphs.

In the years to come, I believe that the 10-year average P/E ratio continues to represent fair value for IDA. This is because the company's growth profile is vastly improved compared to the recent past. IDA's annual forward diluted EPS growth consensus of approximately 8% is more than double its 10-year average annual diluted EPS growth rate of almost 4%.

That should cancel out the impact of a higher 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yield moving forward. In the past 10 years, the average 10-year yield was about 2.5%, according to data from Macrotrends. This implies an investment spread of about 240 basis points between IDA's earnings yield and the average 10-year yield.

The electric utility's growth profile could warrant a narrowing of this investment spread to around half of the historical average. Plug in my expectations of a high-3% 10-year yield in the coming years and this is how I come out to a fair value P/E ratio of 20.5.

As of now, the calendar year 2025 is 45% complete. That leaves another 55% of 2025 and 45% of 2026 yet to come in the next 12 months. This produces a forward 12-month diluted EPS input of $6.07.

Applying this input to my fair value P/E multiple of 20.5, I arrive at a fair value of $124 a share. Compared to the current $115 share price (as of June 12th, 2025), that equates to a 7% discount to fair value.

If IDA meets the growth consensus and reverts to fair value, it could have an 11% upside (11% annualized) through June 2026. The electric utility's annual total return potential by the end of 2030 would also be around 10%.

The Dividend Can Move Higher

IDA's 3% forward dividend yield comes in moderately below the utility sector's forward median yield of 3.5%. This accounts for the C- and D+ grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively.

IDA makes up for the lesser starting income in other aspects, though. The company's diluted EPS payout ratio is positioned to be in the high 50% range for 2025. Per The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal, that's better than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies prefer from the industry. This is behind the Quant System's A grade for overall dividend safety.

IDA has also been busy building a track record as a dividend grower over the years. The electric utility has upped its dividend for 13 consecutive years, which is better than the sector median of 11 years. That warrants an A- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency.

IDA also looks poised to keep raising the dividend in the future. This argument is supported by both a sustainable payout ratio and its high-single-digit annual diluted EPS growth prospects. IDA's massive capital spending plan will likely require it to retain more capital. That's why I think the Quant System's projection of 4.8% annual forward dividend per share growth is realistic (versus 5.2% sector median) if not a percentage point too conservative. This justifies a B+ grade for overall dividend growth from the Quant System.

Risks To Consider

The fundamentals portray IDA as a solid company with a bright future ahead. However, the electric utility has its risks like any other investments.

One notable risk to IDA is that it derives almost all of its revenue from Idaho and the remaining share of revenue from Oregon. An unfavorable rate case decision in the former state could be enough to meaningfully alter the growth thesis.

If the outcome is bad enough, it could lead to credit downgrades from the rating agencies. This could raise IDA's cost of capital and force it to reevaluate its capital spending plans. Separately, an economic slowdown in its service territories or failure to attract major commercial customers like data centers could also hurt growth.

Another risk to the company is the potential that arises from its geographic concentration is the potential for severe weather (i.e., wildfires or floods) to damage its infrastructure. If the damages surpass its commercial insurance coverage, IDA may have to spend out of pocket to rebuild its infrastructure. Regulators would likely allow it to recover these costs through higher rates for customers down the line. Such an event could stretch the electric utility's balance sheet before such an approval, though.

Summary: A Lower-Risk AI Play

IDA is a company that benefits from macro tailwinds like economic development and data centers. Company-specific factors like cheap and readily available access to hydropower also factor into the investment thesis. This is why I expect exceptional diluted EPS growth from the company in the years ahead. The company is also financially stable, with well-laddered debt maturities.

Topping off the buy case, IDA's shares are marginally undervalued. This should help deliver low-double-digit annual total returns for the foreseeable future. That's why I'm reaffirming my buy rating.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  1. Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  2. The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2026, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  3. The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  4. Benchmark-Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 3.31.2025. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Performance Review

  • The Fund (Institutional Class) returned 2.87 percent, outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, by 0.09 percent.
  • Outperformance was driven by duration positioning and yield advantage over the benchmark.
  • Fixed-income spreads widened on escalating trade tensions and rising recessionary fears, detracting from returns.
  • An underweight to investment-grade (IG) corporates and positive selection across corporates in general helped mitigate losses as spreads widened.

Strategy and Positioning

  • The Fund maintained a slight duration overweight versus the benchmark concentrated in the front-to-intermediate part of the yield curve where yields are elevated by historical standards and stand to benefit from monetary policy easing should economic conditions deteriorate.
  • Credit positioning favors higher credit quality forms of carry and shorter duration instruments such as senior tranches within non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), commercial asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs).
  • Increased Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) exposure focused on “production coupon” mortgage pools, which exhibited high carry profiles and wide spreads due to interest rate volatility. We maintained an underweight to IG corporates due to compressed spreads, though selective additions were made during the quarter at attractive valuations.
  • Our higher than normal allocation to Treasurys (though still underweight the benchmark) and Treasury Inflation Protected Securities (TIPS) provides dry powder for future credit opportunities.

QTD Contributors / Detractors

  • Carry contributed 1.4 percent to absolute return and 0.1 percent to relative return.
  • Duration contributed 1.8 percent on an absolute basis and 0.2 percent on a relative basis.
  • Spread widening across corporate and structured credit detracted on absolute and relative basis.

Economic and Market Review

  • Uncertainty about the administration’s policies remains elevated, and many questions remain about how tariff levels will evolve from here. We have moved our outlook for U.S. and global economic growth materially lower. In the U.S., we expect growth will slow to barely positive levels this year.
  • Consumer spending, which has already been decelerating, will likely retrench further as higher prices weigh on real incomes and consumers save more in response to elevated uncertainty. Higher goods prices are likely to hit low income consumers disproportionately, a segment of the population already under stress. Business investment will also suffer, with near-term supply chain disorder expected.
  • We believe the odds of recession have materially increased and are now quite elevated, but our assessment could change. The outlook could improve if negotiations further lower tariff levels, or if rapid progress is made on a tax cut bill that helps support economic growth. In the other direction, additional trade partner retaliation or an even sharper tightening in financial conditions could make a recession more likely.
  • At current tariff levels, we believe core inflation will rise close to 3.5 percent this year. But given the economic slowdown, we anticipate the inflationary pressure from this price shock will fade in 2026.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) is likely to face challenges to both sides of its mandate this year, but given the expectation for a sizable jump in inflation, the Fed will be cautious in responding to the expected economic slowdown, waiting to ease until labor market weakness shows up in the data. We expect that means a cautious approach to rate cuts initially, before the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) responds more forcefully in 2026 and ultimately takes the fed funds rate below 3 percent.
  • A more rapid economic slowdown could accelerate the pace of cuts in 2025. A risk to our view is if measures of longer-term inflation expectations broadly rise, which could increase the weight the FOMC places on defending its inflation mandate. It is a difficult spot for both the Fed and investors, with a nimble approach needed as the policy backdrop rapidly shifts.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors. In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer-term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities. Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk. CLOs bear similar risks to investing in loans directly. Investments in loans involve special types of risks, including credit, interest rate, counterparty, prepayment, liquidity, and valuation risks. Loans are often below investment grade, may be unrated, and typically offer a fixed or floating interest rate. High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility. The fund’s use of leverage, through borrowings or instruments such as derivatives, may cause the fund to be more volatile and riskier than if it had not been leveraged. The more a fund invests in leveraged instruments, the more the leverage will magnify any gains or losses on those investments. Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to many of the same risks as leveraged instruments, such as derivatives. You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS (agency and non-agency). The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index measures the market for USD- denominated non-investment grade, fixed rate, taxable corporate bonds. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch, and S&P is Bal/BB+/ BB+ or below. The index excludes emerging market debt.

data.title: Two Canadian Microcaps Trading At High Free Cash Yields
data.description:
Introduction

I am always looking for companies that trade at a discount to some measure of value. When I say some measure of value, I am recognizing that there are a number of ways to establish value in an oil and gas company. Long-term asset values matter, but these companies can often trade at a substantial discount to these values. EV/EBITDA is another common metric, but one that can be influenced by the amount of debt on the books.

The one we are going to use in this analysis is free cash flow, divided by the enterprise value-EV, or the equity value, which is free cash yield-FCY. The higher the number, the more cash the company is generating, and conceptually, the sounder the investment rationale.

There are two companies I've been buying at post-Liberation Day lows and have been rewarded with a 40% rally in one, and a 15% rally in the other in just a few weeks. What's impressive to me is that these companies are still trading in double-digit FCYs after this rally. The companies are Saturn Oil and Gas, (OTCQX:OILSF) and Hemisphere Energy, (OTCQX:HMENF). I have discussed both of these companies previously and directed readers to those older reports for a deep analysis of these companies, here and here.

What's the macro for these companies?

The Canadian E&P industry is going through a bit of upheaval. For decades, the country's operators have viewed the U.S. as their primary and customer of last resort. The tariff dust-up with the U.S. has rubbed their noses the wrong way to say the least (not taking a position here, just stating a fact), and there is a push to expand their marketing optionality to other countries. However, the tariff push and pull turns out a sleeping giant has been awakened.

A couple of years ago there weren't any real options for them, but the arrival of the Transmountain Express in 2024 changed all of that, as did the start-up of LNG Canada. Add to this, there is an emerging awareness that while Canadian and U.S. energy interests are inextricably linked, there need to be additional export options for the country. Canada may be building new pipelines if the political rhetoric of their recent election becomes a reality.

In short, we see a bright future for Canada's E&P industry and think that over time the market will begin to properly appreciate the assets they hold. Ok, let's look at Saturn and the Hemisphere.

Saturn Oil and Gas

My last article on Saturn came out just after the company's shares fell 40% post-Liberation Day. I gave it a strong buy in my Investing Group, The Daily Drilling Report, and again when I took the article to the public side a few days later after Liberation Day on April 2nd. It was a great call, as shares have rallied nearly 100% since, and are now above the pre-Liberation Day peak. Is there more to come? We think so, as we see Saturn as being undervalued.

Seeking Alpha authors rate the company as a buy, but the coverage is pretty sparse, with just two authors maintaining coverage. Wall Street has not yet discovered the stock.

Saturn has several core operations centers in Alberta and Saskatchewan provinces. Their focus is light oil and condensates, with those providing 82% of their output.

Two of Saturn's core operating areas are low decline, conventional production that is enhanced in late-life with waterfloods in Central and South East Saskatchewan. The other is in Central Alberta, focusing on Cardium and Montney oil and liquids-rich intervals that are high-return stack plays with multiple light oil zones. The company has established a good track record of growing production organically and through strategic acquisitions, like the one that brought the Flatlake and Battrum properties into the fold.

Saturn released their Q-1, 2025 report on May 11th and just KILLED IT. With the production of 42K BOEPD—a record, and then went on to beat analyst expectations of OCF of $158 mm (CAD), and free cash of $58 mm (CAD). As noted above, Saturn is trading at a 35% free cash yield. That can't last, with the uplift we are seeing in oil prices presently.

The company has been incorporating and optimizing the recently acquired assets at Flat Lake and Battrum and reducing opex expenses. Costs have been reduced by 13%, driving a cost savings of $7.5 million-$15 mm on a full-year basis, realized in the second half of 2024 alone. It has also seen good results from its Middale and Spearfish multilateral wells, with some achieving a 50% uplift from estimates.

Saturn has been focusing free cash on a couple of areas—debt reduction and share repurchases, primarily targeting repayment of US$16.3 million in debt on their senior notes. On their NCIB, $5.8 million was used for the repurchase of 2.8 million common shares for cancellation at a weighted average price of $2.08. Just as this piece was being written, a new tender under the NCIB was announced.

Saturn has a strong balance sheet, with its substantial debt covered by cash flow. Liquidity at year-end of approximately $230 million, comprised of $80 million cash and $150 million undrawn credit facility.

Guidance

The company is guiding to full-year production between 38-40K BOEPD with free funds flow between $115-$165 mm for the year. Rising crude prices—if they last, certainly put the higher number in their sights.

Company Filings

Hemisphere Energy

This company came to my attention a couple of years ago on a stock screen. It had rallied hard from about a buck to the $1.25 level. I was pretty enthused about the company after speaking with the CEO, Dan Simmons, about their plans, and gave it a strong buy in this article. The company's polymer flood strategy-which I discussed at length, and substantial-9.3 mm BOE, PDP reserve base supported the notion that the company is undervalued long term.

In my original coverage, I suggested the company might see further appreciation toward the $1.66 level. For a while after the article came out, it looked like it might reach that target in October of last year. But then everything "oil" fell apart in Q-4, and the shares took a tumble from $1.48 down to $1.17.

It is difficult to know when the share price of HMENF will truly reflect the value of its reserves, and it's not something I am especially concerned with at present. The company has started the pilot project at Marsden we discussed in the last article. If that proves to be a success, reserves will leap higher. As the company notes, we should have an idea by the end of the year.

I am a dividend income investor primarily, and HMENF has been ticking that box quite nicely with regular payments and two special payments during the time I've owned it. While the yield on cost of 5.4% isn't spectacular, their free cash yield of 22% puts them in the all-star column. Management objectives seem to be closely aligned with shareholder interests—steady development vs. rapid growth, and it makes sense as management controls 17% of the equity.

My opinion varies from other Seeking Alpha analysts who rate the company as a sell. It would be fair to note the company is a little under-covered on this platform. Wall Street is more sanguine with a strong buy rating.

In May, the company issued its Q-1 earnings release, meeting EPS estimates of $0.06, and beating revenue estimates by $1.34 mm (CAD). Improvement in WTI pricing—that is a reference point for the heavy crude the company produces, provided a catalyst for the shares, and they moved higher to the present $1.35 USD per share. A lot of positive sequential and year-over-year metrics were discussed in the release. Some notes from the release are shown below-

HMENF cranked out record quarterly production of 3,833 BOEPD of 99% heavy oil), a 21% increase over the same period of last year. That production generated $27.3 million in revenue, a 30% increase over the first quarter of 2024. Operating and transportation costs totaled out to $14.63/BOE.

Such low costs delivered an operating netback of $17.0 million, or $49.27/BOE. Quarterly adjusted funds flow from operations of $12.7 million, or $36.83/BOE, a 26% increase over the first quarter of 2024. HMENF generated a free funds flow of $11.5 million, or $0.12 per share.

$2.4 million, or $0.025 per share, in dividends were paid to shareholders during the quarter. Additionally, the company repurchased and canceled 709,700 shares for $1.3 million under the Company's Normal Course Issuer Bid-NCIB.

HMENF ended Q-1 with a working capital balance of $14.1 million, an improvement of $9.9 CAD million over the same period in 2024.

The debt-free company trades at 2.2 EV/EBITDA and less than half its PDP reserves NAV.

Company filings

Risks

These companies are trading just above the mid-point of their one-year price ranges on the strength of May's crude rally. So if the drum-beats of war subside, we can expect the current strength in crude prices to weaken, and take shares of OILSF and HMENF down a peg. That said, we don't expect a big drop—absent a price collapse.

Your takeaway

Saturn has become my largest position in a number of shares since making a big buy around mid-April. My only regret is not buying more at the $1.03ish level. I am deep, deep in the green on Saturn and very satisfied with what they've told us to expect this year, including their free cash yield of 35%. Let's say they do hit the $165 mm in free cash this year. That would imply an EBITDA of about $400-450 mm. To keep their multiple of 2.2 the shares would need to blast through $2.00 per share and perhaps tap $5.00 on the shoulder.

You can tell I like Hemisphere. Their low decline, and low cost of reserves enable full cycle returns to shareholders, and that's the kind of company I want to own. When might we expect some sustainable growth? The current 2.2X EV/EBITDA multiple for a stock with a 5.4% dividend yield, and a 22% free cash yield just seems too low.

Summing up, I think Saturn and Hemisphere remain solidly in the strong buy column a buy for their robust production strategies, and double-digit free cash yields. Both have demonstrated the ability to generate profits under less-than-optimal macros, making them suitable long-term investments.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Assessing Strategy's New Perpetual Stride Preferred Shares
data.description:
After purchasing Bitcoin (BTC-USD) at a blistering clip for much of April and May, Strategy (MSTR) has slowed down the company's purchases of BTC during the month of June. Through June 11th, Strategy has reported buying just 1,750 BTC at a total cost of $185 million:

For just about any other company in the public equity markets, $185 million in just a couple of weeks would be significant. But for Strategy, this is perhaps underwhelming, given the company has averaged about $4 billion per month going back to September. What is also interesting, in my view, is that both of the announced purchases have come with funding through Strategy's preferred stock offerings via the MicroStrategy Incorporated 10.00% SER A PERPETUAL STRIFE PFD STK (STRF) and MicroStrategy Incorporated 8.00% SERIES A PERPETUAL STRIKE PFD (STRK) rather than through the company's common stock.

Simultaneous to these preferred share-driven BTC acquisitions, Strategy announced the offering and pricing of a third perpetual preferred share, this latest one is the Perpetual Stride Preferred Stock (NASDAQ:STRD). In this article, I'll detail how STRD is different from Strategy's previous two preferred share offerings and assess whether income investors should consider adding STRD to their portfolios.

STRD Offering Details

On June 6th, Strategy announced the offering of 2,500,000 shares of STRD perpetual preferred shares with a yield of 10%. Subsequent to the offering, Strategy sold 11,764,700 shares at an offering price of $85 per share. The STRD offering raised $1 billion for the company, with expected net proceeds of $979.7 million after underwriting commissions. Like the two other perpetual preferred shares, STRD has a liquidation preference of $100. Which means the effective yield on STRD would actually be closer to 11.3% at the closing price of $88.35 on June 11th assuming Strategy declares the dividend and pays shareholders. That last point is the critical difference between STRD and both STRK/STRF.

Comparison of Strategy Preferred Stocks

Unlike STRK and STRF, STRD is not a mandatory dividend. This makes STRD the riskiest of the three offerings, but it's a terrific way to raise capital from Strategy's vantage, since it isn't dilutive to common stockholders. The latter of which saw shares outstanding grow by over 50% year over year as of the quarter ended March, with even more dilution through the ATM to fund BTC purchases subsequent to Q1.

Still, assuming Strategy wants to ever be able to raise capital again in this market, I would expect the company to declare the STRD dividend each quarter. The breakdown of the key differences between the shares as I see it is below:

Share Comparison STRD STRF STRK
Coupon 10% 10% 8%
Cumulative? No Yes Yes
Mandatory Dividend? No Yes Yes
MSTR Convertible? No No 10:1
Preferred Seniority Rank 3 1 2
Liquidation Preference $100 $100 $100
Share Price Close as of 6/11/25 $88.35 $104.70 $106.72
Effective Yield 11.32% 9.55% 7.50%

Source: Strategy

From where I sit, Strategy has actually done a pretty incredible job of creating preferred share structures that appeal to different investor types. All of these preferred shares are ultimately backed by the company's Bitcoin on the balance sheet. But one need not believe in the future price appreciation of BTC to find these shares appealing.

Given the seniority rank of STRF compared to the other preferred stocks, I view STRF as the lowest-risk and most suitable for income investors. The shares have a mandatory dividend that are cumulative. Meaning, if Strategy misses a payment, STRF shareholders are still owed the payout. Since STRF is not convertible to MSTR shares, the upside in STRF is more limited than that of STRK in the event Bitcoin rallies significantly and drags MSTR with it. But even at a 9.55% effective yield, STRF is quite attractive given Strategy's collateralization ratio.

STRK is perhaps more suitable for investors who care less about the effective yield and more about the total return of the shares. At a $1,000 MSTR share price, STRK becomes interesting on the capital appreciation potential given the 10:1 convertibility to the common stock. However, purely as an income product, I think STRK is less appealing than STRF due to the seniority rank and lower effective yield.

STRD is perhaps the most interesting preferred stock of the three offerings because it's the highest risk and currently has the highest yield. For income investors who like sure things, I'd probably avoid this one. But for income-investors who have a higher risk-tolerance, I think STRD is worth a look. The biggest reason for that - aside from Strategy's collateralization ratio - is that the market has already taken the more senior preferred shares above liquidation preference. So the effective yields when buying those funds on secondary are actually lower than the 10% and 8% coupons from the offering. Thus, if you still want a Strategy preferred that yields 10%, STRD is your only option currently.

Can Strategy Actually Pay These Shareholders?

Ultimately, the investment case for any of these preferred stock shares lies in the ability Strategy has to actually pay shareholders back. At current Bitcoin price levels, my view is that there is low risk in holding these shares today. However, that view is predicated on the notion that Strategy exhibits a willingness to lock in some of the company's unrealized Bitcoin gains and pay its preferred share dividends with the proceeds of those sales.

Even if Strategy stops raising capital through the preferred instruments going forward, the company has an annualized coupon obligation of over $300 million based on my calculation of the combined outstanding shares:

Preferred Payouts STRK STRF STRD
Shares 11,306,243 9,412,070 11,764,700
Annual Coupon Obligation $90,449,944 $94,120,700 $117,647,000

Source: Strategy filings, Analyst's calculations

Of course, Strategy could simply not pay the $118 million STRD dividends over the next 12 months. But again, I think doing so would irreparably damage the company's ability to raise capital. My view is it's essentially a mathematical certainty that Strategy will have to start selling Bitcoin to pay these preferred obligations. Keep in mind, the company's software business loses money and cannot support these preferred share payouts.

After a gross profit of $326 million on a trailing twelve-month basis, Strategy's bottom-line goes negative when factoring in the combined $392 million on SG&A and R&D expenses. So again, Strategy cannot pay these dividends from the operations of the software business. The company either has to keep raising from new investors to pay the old ones (there is a word for this) or it has to realize gains in BTC appreciation. My vote is the latter.

Major Risk

Of course, the risk here is that Strategy is one of the biggest BTC holders in the market at this juncture. If the company starts selling, investment demand through the spot ETF products could sour and Strategy could take the market down essentially by itself. But given the current market value of Bitcoin, I think longing STRD is worth the risk given Strategy has $11.5 billion in convertible debt and preferred share obligations against $63 billion in Bitcoin holdings.

Closing Summary

In my opinion, STRF is hands-down the best choice for income-focused investors who want a high-yield offering with some margin of safety. With STRF, the dividends are mandatory, cumulative, and are senior to the other two offerings. Meaning, if Bitcoin goes into a deep bear market and Strategy is forced to sell BTC to make preferred payments, STRF shareholders are paid back before STRK and STRD shareholders.

While STRD is clearly the riskiest of the three offerings because there is no guarantee of any dividends at all, I do expect Strategy to pay them and believe the market will bring STRD shares more in line with the liquidation preference of $100 as it did with both STRK and STRF; each of which now trade at premiums to liquidation preference. Thus, at a share price currently trading under $89, I think STRD is somewhat easy to justify on the potential price appreciation alone.

As I noted in my last coverage of MSTR, I'm personally long STRF. I am now also long STRD to a smaller degree. My approach to STRD is quite simple; I'm long the shares at $88 and plan to sell closer to $95-96. At that point, I'd entertain rolling proceeds into STRF for the mandatory payment. I remain of the view that Strategy's Bitcoin flywheel could have devastating consequences in a Bitcoin bear market, and I view STRF shares as insurance against my BTC position. The price of that insurance is the spread between Bitcoin capital gains that I may miss out on and the 11% effective yield at my STRF cost basis.

data.title: Ares Capital: I Overestimated This One
data.description:
Back in late October of 2023, one company, specifically a BDC (business development company), that I decided to look into was Ares Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:ARCC). I initially found myself attracted to the hefty yield that the company offered that, at that time, stood at about 10%. And what I found was a thriving company that had attractive fundamentals and a history of value creation for shareholders. I ultimately decided, based on my findings, to rate the business a ‘strong buy’, which is the rating that I assign firms that, I believe, are likely to significantly outperform the broader market for the foreseeable future.

Unfortunately, things did not progress from that point in the way that I would have hoped. To be clear, the performance the company achieved was far from bad. The overall return for investors has been 36.5%. But that actually fell short of the 42.6% appreciation enjoyed by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. Looking at the picture again today, the yield is a bit lower. Having said that, management continues to do a fine job. Because of broader market conditions, and the lower yield, which now stands at 8.65%, I will say that I am a little less enchanted by the company than I was back then. Because of this, I have decided to downgrade it to a very respectful ‘buy’ rating.

Still Solid

For investors that are looking for attractive yields, there are three main routes that can be considered. Certainly, the most popular would be to invest in REITs (real estate investment trusts). Next in line would be to invest in the pipeline space, especially when it comes to those that are structured as MLPs. But a third route is to consider investing in a BDC. In essence, a BDC is a specialty finance company that takes the capital it raises from the market and allocates that capital toward investments in other opportunities. The objective here is not only to diversify operations, but also to benefit from capturing higher returns by investing that capital in the companies that otherwise would only have access to higher cost private financing.

In the case of Ares Capital Corporation, the company seeks to generate income and capital appreciation through debt and equity investments. Most of its capital is allocated toward middle market firms operating in the US. Management describes this category of company as those that generate annual EBITDA of between $10 million and $250 million. This is quite a wide range. But considering how large publicly traded companies can get, it's really not all that significant of a range.

Most of the company’s capital is put toward first lien senior secured loans and toward second lien senior secured loans. It does invest some capital into subordinated debt, as well as, at times, equity and preferred equity. As of the end of the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, about 58% of the company's assets were dedicated to first lien senior secured loans. Another 6% was allocated towards second lien senior secured loans. All combined, that comes out to roughly 64%. The rest of its capital is allocated as shown in the image above.

The emphasis on secured debt means that, in theory, the overall risk profile that the company has to contend with is quite low. However, management is not content to diversify in this way only. In fact, the company is diversified in other ways as well. For instance, the largest issuer concentration that it has involves Ivy Hill Asset Management, which accounts for 7% of its total investment portfolio. But even this one doesn't really count. That's because it is the company's wholly owned portfolio business that manages 20 different investment vehicles and that boasted total assets under management at the end of last year of $12.8 billion.

Another 4% of its portfolio is dedicated to its Senior Direct Lending Program, which is a joint venture that the company has with Varagon Capital Partners. This particular entity makes first lien senior secured loans to US middle market companies. Beyond this, its next largest exposure is just 2% of its portfolio. And in fact, once you move outside of the top 15 entities that it invests in, you end up with the other 75% of its portfolio that is spread quite wide. In fact, its average holding accounts for just 0.2% of total assets. And its largest, excluding those that are affiliated with it, accounts for just 1.6%.

Management has also made sure to diversify the company based on industry. Admittedly, it does have a lot of exposure to the software and services space, with 24% of its assets allocated to that category. This is followed up by the 13% that involves healthcare equipment and services, and the 10% that includes commercial and professional services. The company also invests in a wide array of other opportunities, including insurance, consumer services, sports, media, and entertainment, independent power and renewables, financial services, food and beverage companies, and more. All told, the company has investments spread across 33 different industries.

In order to get a good picture of this business, there are some different ways that we should look at it compared to most other types of enterprises. In the chart above, you can see some important metrics that I laid out. The blue columns on the chart represent the sum of profits and realized gains and losses that the company has achieved over the last three completed fiscal years and for the first quarter of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. The general trend here has been toward higher profits and realized gains. At the same time, management has been able to step up the size of the company's distribution. Last year, for instance, it paid out $1.14 billion to shareholders. That was up from the $912 million reported two years earlier. And for the first quarter of this year, the $308 million in distributions that the company doled out exceeded, by a comfortable margin, the $270 million paid out last year in the first quarter.

This track record has been positive. But of course, there are other metrics that we should be paying attention to as well. In the chart above, for instance, you can see the sum of excess profits and realized gains and losses over what the company distributed. This is basically a tally of any additional value accrued to the firm that has not been paid out. You can also see, as part of this, the cumulative profits, plus any realized gains and losses, that have accrued in recent years. And you can also see the cumulative distribution that the company has been able to accomplish. These are all solid indicators and demonstrate that, even while paying out a hefty yield, the business has been successful in growing overall shareholder value.

While the playground that Ares Capital Corporation dabbles in might seem small and possessing only limited upside, management disagrees. In fact, management estimates that the addressable market that it can focus on is worth about $5.4 trillion. $3 trillion of this market opportunity comes from US firms with revenues of between $100 million and $1 billion. The other $2.4 trillion comes from companies with revenues in excess of $1 billion. It certainly helps that significant consolidation in the banking industry has reduced access to capital for many firms. According to management, over the last 44 years, ending in 2024, there's been a roughly 70% reduction in the number of banks in the US. A continuation of this trend, combined with a general growth in the size of the economy, should allow this industry to continue expanding for the foreseeable future.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I think that while shares of Ares Capital Corporation have performed well, they have fallen short of what I expected them to. I consider this a failure as a result. Having said that, management continues to demonstrate that the enterprise is a high-quality operator and is capable of distributing significant amounts of cash. Because the yield of the business is lower than it was previously, and because I am worried about the state of the economy, with a recession likely around the corner, I do believe that pulling back a bit from a ‘strong buy’ rating to a ‘buy’ rating makes sense at this time.

data.title: Mandalay Resources: Greater Visibility For Shareholders In Combination With Alkane Resources
data.description:
A Hold Rating for Shares of Mandalay Resources Corporation

With this analysis, our first for this stock, we would like to say that Mandalay Resources Corporation (OTCQB:MNDJF) deserves a Hold rating, in the sense that portfolios with Mandalay have growth potential and that volatility offers them an incredible opportunity to expand on that potential.

The growth opportunity is described in the following Seeking Alpha news dated April 28, 2025, at 7:28 AM ET:

Alkane Resources to buy Canada's Mandalay Resources in $358M deal

Throughout this discussion, we will highlight why we believe Mandalay shareholders will realise impressive benefits upon completion of the A$559.1 million (≈ US$358 million) all-share transaction between the two companies. Under this transaction, Mandalay shareholders will receive 7.875 ordinary shares of Alkane Resources Ltd (OTCPK:ALKEF). Mandalay and Alkane shareholders will each hold 45% and 55%, respectively, of the total outstanding shares of the new, combined company. Based on the market price of the two stocks at the time of this writing (MNDJF: US$ 3.81/share vs. ALKEF: US$ 0.46/share), Mandalay shareholders would receive 8,283 common shares, which ALKEF must issue in exchange for each MNDJF common share. However, under the agreement, Mandalay shareholders will only receive 7.875 common shares. The market value of the US-listed MNDJF shares is quite high relative to the number of common shares they will receive as part of the all-stock merger agreement. To be clear, Mandalay shareholders will receive the number of newly issued shares from Alkane at a fixed ratio of 7.875:1 for each Mandalay share held, regardless of the share price on the stock market from now until the completion of the merger in Q3 2025. However, if the strategy is to own a larger stake in the newly merged company, we believe it is always better to preserve as much capital as possible while the investor builds up their position in Mandalay, which can then be exchanged for newly issued Alkane shares upon the material execution of the all-share deal transaction. This strategy must consider two dynamics, ultimately shifting from a "hold" stance to a more bullish stance if: A) the exchange ratio trend is equal to or lower than 7.875:1; B) Mandalay shares are undervalued relative to past trends. Investors should therefore wait until the cyclical fluctuations of Mandalay's share price are reflected in both dynamics simultaneously. In terms of the newly issued shares they will receive from Alkane under the all-stock deal transaction, investors would not overpay for Mandalay shares and potentially use a price in line with a "buy-the-dip" approach. Given the volatility of the gold price, it may be possible to temporarily depart from the "Hold" rating and pursue the goal of a greater future participation in the larger, newly merged company through the strength of the position in Mandalay Resources' US-listed stock in the pre-merger phase.

Investors had the opportunity to profit from the "dip" (technically, a significant decline) in Mandalay's share price around May 19. Due to cyclicality, which correlates positively with gold price fluctuations, Mandalay shares traded significantly lower than when the all-stock transaction was announced on April 28, despite the expected benefits of the merger with Alkane.

The positive correlation between Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) and the Mandalay Resources share price means that on average the securities move in the same direction (up-up or down-down), regardless of returns, which can also vary widely over a given period, as correlation has nothing to do with the returns the securities can generate. The positive correlation in the chart above is described by a solid yellow curve line that is above zero (in positive territory) for almost the entire observed period.

Strict adherence to the "7.875:1 maximum, plus price dip" strategy requires a disciplined trading strategy that allows investors to mitigate the potential negative consequences of Mandalay Resources' low liquidity stock. According to the trading section of the Seeking Alpha website, the Average Volume (3 months) of publicly traded U.S. shares of Mandalay Resources is 35,794 out of a total of 94.78 million shares outstanding (Ticker).

Mandalay-Alkane Merger: What Changes for Shareholders

The merger of Mandalay and Alkane is expected to create a larger and more efficient company with a stronger presence in jurisdictions favourable for exploration and mining activities, greater visibility in the financial markets, increased stock market popularity, and a significantly better position to benefit from the sharp rise in the price of gold.

In this announcement, Globe Newswire describes both companies.

About Alkane Resources

Alkane Resources intends to grow to become one of Australia's leading multi-mine gold and copper producers. The Company's current gold production is from the Tomingley Gold mine in New South Wales, which has been operating since 2014 and has operating plans extending beyond 2030.

Alkane also owns a major porphyry gold-copper deposit discovered at Boda-Kaiser in 2019. Exploration is ongoing and economics demonstrated in a 2024 scoping study. Alkane's gold interests extend throughout Australia, with strategic investments in other gold exploration and aspiring mining companies.

For existing shareholders and potential investors of Mandalay Resources, the Globe Newswire describes their business as follows:

About Mandalay Resources

Mandalay Resources is a Canadian-based natural resource company with producing assets in Australia (Costerfield gold-antimony mine) and Sweden (Björkdal gold mine). The Company is focused on growing its production and reducing costs to generate significant positive cashflow. Mandalay is committed to operating safely and in an environmentally responsible manner, while developing a high level of community and employee engagement.

Production Expanded, Large Presence in Mining-friendly Jurisdiction

Mandalay produces not only gold (about 80% of total sales), 20,376 ounces were produced in the most recent quarter of Q1-2025, but also antimony, 161 tonnes in Q1 2025, resulting in 25,779 ounces of gold equivalent produced in Q1 2025.

The merger is subject to shareholder approval of both companies and regulatory approval. Upon completion of the merger, Mandalay Resources shareholders will no longer own the targeted production of 85,000 to 95,000 ounces of gold equivalent in 2025 at an All-In Sustaining Cost of US$1,795 to US$1,975 per ounce of gold equivalent, in accordance with the company's full-year 2025 guidance.

Instead, they will be invested in a merged company with a production target of approximately 160,000 ounces of gold equivalent in 2025 and the potential to increase this production to over 180,000 ounces by 2026 at a lower All-In Sustaining Cost per ounce of gold equivalent. The enlarged company is expected to produce the metals in 2025 at a profit margin defined by an All-In-Sustaining Cost ("AISC") of approximately A$2,750 (approximately US$1,760) per ounce. The operation is expected to become so cost-effective that lower AISCs of approximately A$2,160/oz Au Eq. or approximately US$1,420/oz Au Eq. are forecast for the following year, 2026.

The merged company will operate three producing mines: Tomingley (Alkane Resources Limited) and Costerfield (Mandalay Resources Corporation) for gold in Australia, and Björkdal (Mandalay Resources Corporation) for gold and antimony in Sweden, in prime jurisdictions.

The Mining Risk Heatmap from Sprott—a global asset manager with extensive experience in physical investments in metals and minerals, as well as close relationships and expertise in the mining industry—shows that Australia and Sweden have a dark blue code. This means these regions have a very mining and exploration-friendly jurisdiction and environment, resulting in very low, if not any, investment risk.

Diversified Metal Portfolio in Light of the Rosy Outlook

The merger proponents aim to diversify the raw materials portfolio, which will be conducive to future economic development.

Gold Outlook

The new company will produce a larger amount of gold, thereby being better positioned to capitalise on the growing trend that sees the precious metal as a safe haven against the negative winds in a scenario where turbulence will become more and more regular and pose a serious risk to portfolios. As a benchmark for the gold price, the Gold Spot Price is trading at US$ 3,361.09 per troy ounce, which at the time of writing represents an increase of ≈ 28.58% year-to-date and ≈ 45.42% over the past 12 months, mainly due to strong demand for safe-haven assets. In fact, as stated in the Equity Insider News Commentary issued on behalf of RUA GOLD Inc. (RUA:CA) on May 27, 2025, via PR Newswire and reported by Seeking Alpha:

Safe-haven demand, rate cut expectations, and fresh volatility tied to US President Donald Trump's tariff warnings have sent bullion prices soaring, prompting Citi to raise its short-term gold target to $3,500. Bank of America followed by increasing its gold exposure in Q4, citing a bullish shift in underlying market conditions.

[…]

Veteran investor Rob McEwen and billionaire John Paulson both see $5,000 gold within reach, while JPMorgan recently projected a potential path to $6,000 if even a sliver of U.S.-held foreign assets are reallocated toward bullion.

Antimony Outlook

Demand for antimony is strengthening as economies around the world invest heavily in expanding their weapons production capacity in the face of growing global conflicts and challenges.

Based on the previous agreement on the further development of Iran's nuclear energy program, the following states parties to this agreement, the USA, Great Britain, the EU (Germany and France), but also China and Russia, are among the largest weapons producers in the world.

According to SIPRI reported by Trading Economics, military spending in 2024 reached an all-time high of US$997,309 million in the US, an all-time high of US$88,458.50 million in Germany, an all-time high of US$64,675.00 million in France, an all-time high of US$81,763.20 million in the UK, an all-time high of US$313,658.30 million in China and an all-time high of US$148,967.30 million in Russia.

Antimony prices are forecast to keep rising due to a gain in the momentum of the demand as a result of not only massive investments in defense production, but also to the use of this raw material in the green energy and electronics industries. The most popular applications of this raw material are flame retardants, batteries, and semiconductors. As Chinese exports of antimony are subject to increased scrutiny, particularly in the wake of a global trade conflict, leading to supply chain disruptions, among other things, supply becomes insufficient to meet demand. This is leading to price pressure. The antimony price—estimated at $20,000 per ton in Mandalay (production and cost forecast for the full year 2025)—has already scaled up two and a half times this year, and speculators see no end to this growth trend in the antimony price per ton. In fact, CarbonCredits.Com writes:

The surge comes from a supply shortage in China, Russia, and Southeast Asia. At the same time, demand is rising in the solar sector, fire retardants, and military uses.

Recent posts on X show that the shortage is still affecting availability. Prices have reached $51,500 per ton in 2025. Some market speculation suggests that prices could reach $100,000 per ton.

The website carboncredits-dot.com reports the following chart on the "Antimony Price" of the S&P Global

Improved Profitability

Assuming the bullish gold price forecast is correct, and the yellow metal sells for $4,000 or even $5,000 per ounce in the coming quarters, Mandalay's profit margin, which starts to build on an AISC of $1,795 to $1,975 per ounce, is one thing. The profit margin achieved by the combined company, with a remarkably lower AISC of $1,760 per ounce in 2025 or even $1,420 per ounce in 2026, is another matter entirely. The impact on the new company's stock price promises to be incredibly positive, as rising profitability is a key factor in the sentiment of North American equity markets.

For the first three months of 2025, Mandalay Resources shareholders benefited from a 65% year-on-year jump in adjusted EBITDA to US$44.1 million and a 151% year-on-year jump in consolidated net income to US$14.8 million, supported by consolidated revenue of US$78.1 million, a 41% year-on-year increase.

Mandalay Resources achieved an astonishing boost in profitability by producing 20,376 ounces of gold and 161 tonnes of antimony in Q1-2025. This was supported by a 35.3% year-over-year increase in the gold margin (average realised gold price/oz less All-in sustaining costs/oz gold eq. produced) to US$1,042 in Q1-2025. It's easy to imagine how the combined company's profitability could evolve if 180,000 ounces are produced in 2026, supported by a margin of US$3,580/oz gold eq. This margin is absolutely possible if veteran investors and billionaires consider a spot price of US$5,000 for gold to be within reach.

Not to mention the impact of the electronics hype and the arms race-driven antimony on future business: Mandalay Resources produced 161 tonnes and sold 196 tonnes in the first quarter of 2025 at an average antimony price of $34,923 per tonne (up 152.64% year-on-year). It's easy to imagine the impact that the Costerfield mine's projected annual antimony production of around 2,800 tonnes will have on the combined company's profitability.

Seeking Alpha reports that we have already:

seen prices surge 250% in 2024 following China's ban and an ongoing global supply shortage.

Recently, the price of antimony has already reached $50,000 per ton, and speculators expect it to reach around $100,000 per ton, according to the aforementioned website CarbonCredits.Com.

Mandalay-Alkane Merger: Greater Visibility for Shares on More Markets

The aforementioned Globe Newswire adds:

Following completion of the Transaction, the combined company will continue operating as "Alkane Resources", remain listed on the ASX and is seeking a listing on the TSX exchange.

The enhanced popularity due to the potential listing on more exchanges will benefit Mandalay's current shareholders. Should they currently complain about the lack of popularity due to limited daily trading volume, their shares will likely have a greater market presence after the merger and will be brought to the attention of a larger and more diverse community of market participants. In fact, the aforementioned Globe Newswire also writes:

Improved capital market positioning anticipated to drive valuation re-rate. The combined company is expected to benefit from a primary ASX listing valuation re-rate driven by GDXJ and potential ASX 300 index inclusion. Further value should be unlocked through greater trading liquidity, a larger free-float and a more diverse shareholder base as the combined company continues to grow.

Mandalay Resources shares are currently affected by low trading volumes, but in our view, with higher daily trading volumes, the combined company's shares will be more sensitive to market sentiment, affecting prices, and ultimately, this will also serve as an incentive to mobilise more analysts to cover the stock. Ratings will follow each other much more frequently, and not sporadically as now, and report on the characteristics of a portfolio of metals activity that is consistent with the future economic project. The metals that the merged company will produce, including the projects to develop and install copper production, will be in high demand as we seek to grow through greater environmental awareness, sustainability, and safety, and to be protected from disruptions or potential negative external influences.

The Financial Condition of the Combined Business

The aforementioned Globe Newswire adds:

Creates a powerful platform with a shared vision for continued growth. Robust balance sheet with a proforma cash balance of A$188 / C$167 million 3 at 31 March 2025 to pursue organic and inorganic growth.

With a larger size of business and greater visibility in the financial markets, particularly among analysts, the combined company will also have easier access to debt capital than Alkane, potentially by expanding its existing banking relationships. Mandalay Resources Corporation maintains a debt-free balance sheet, while Alkane Resources has total debt of $38.8 million. This resulted in interest expenses of $1 million over the last 12 months, more than offset by operating income of $14.9 million over the last 12 months.

The start of a new economic cycle is leading to a cooling of borrowing costs, as both the Bank of Canada and the Reserve Bank of Australia ("RBA") are cutting interest rates. This will make it easier to raise the necessary capital to expand gold production and support future developments in copper production.

The aforementioned Globe Newswire adds:

The combined company plans to continue to invest in exploration at all three of its producing mines and pursue other growth opportunities in addition to the Boda-Kaiser copper-gold project which adds future production potential with its significant scale.

A scoping study for Alkane Resources' Boda-Kaiser copper-gold project in New South Wales examined three possible scenarios, including mining and processing 20 million, 10 million, and 5 million tonnes of ore per year. The first scenario is likely to be chosen due to the metals' far more favourable price environment than previously thought and the potential to deliver significantly greater value; it would be a real shame not to capitalise on the first scenario. In the largest production scenario, the Boda-Kaiser copper-gold complex produces an average of 225,350 gold equivalent ounces annually over a 17-year mine life at an AISC of A$1,901.8/oz Au Eq. (or approximately US$1,242.44/ounce at the time of writing). This would make the Boda-Kaiser copper/gold complex a serious candidate for low-cost production, as AISCs are close to US$ 1,500/ounce worldwide, according to the World Gold Council chart.

The estimated pre-tax internal rate of return (IRR) of 24% was calculated based on a long-term gold price of A$3,500 per ounce and a copper price of A$15,000 per tonne (or approximately US$2,279 per ounce of gold and US$9,767.13 per tonne of copper at the time of writing). At a current gold price of US$3,361.09 per troy ounce and a copper futures price (HG1:COM) of US$4.82 per pound, or approximately US$10,626 per tonne, the IRR is significantly higher, between 30 and 35%. However, even at 24%, the Boda-Kaiser copper/gold complex is considered a financially sound project, as we have previously seen mineral metals projects with IRRs in the 15 to 25 percent range.

Not to mention future prices: The price of gold is heading towards US$ 4,000/ounce or even US$ 5,000/ounce, and the price of copper is on the trajectory to spike 4 times from current levels in the next few years, according to hedge fund manager Pierre Andurand's opinion last year. Copper is a key metal for electrification programs, the rollout of electric vehicles, the development of technologies for harnessing renewable energy sources, and the expansion of data centres with AI software. Given this strong demand, supply will struggle to keep pace.

The Boda Kaiser copper-gold project in New South Wales requires an investment of A$ 1.783 million (approximately US$1,160.99 million at the time of writing). We expect it will take some time to secure the necessary cash collateral to successfully obtain the required borrowings. The most realistic scenario is a phased approach to the Boda Kaiser copper-gold project. We draw this conclusion based on our previous analysis of Harmony Gold Mining Company Limited's (HMY) US$1.03 billion deal to fully acquire MAC Copper Limited (MTAL). It is true that MAC Copper's only asset at the CSA copper mine in New South Wales, Australia, is merely copper production of 41,000 tonnes by 2024, or potentially >50,000 tonnes per year by 2026, with a reserve life of more than 12 years, while Boda-Kaiser Copper-Gold also consists of surging gold bullion. However, while the bridge financing of up to US$1.25 billion that HMY can obtain from branches of Citibank, JPMorgan Chase Bank and Macquarie Bank Limited to finance the acquisition is secured by HMY's cash balance of US$1.14 billion, Mandalay and Alkane instead have combined cash and ST investments of approximately US$110 million as of March 31, 2025.

But the nature of the merger also makes it clear that the companies will need time to raise the cash required, and on which advance the financing applications, as it is evident that capital is lacking at present, or that project development will be gradual, proceeding step by step as funds are available. The merger will be carried out through an all-stock transaction. While such transactions are generally frowned upon by investors due to the risk of share dilution, in the case of the merger of Mandalay Resources with Alkane Resources, the all-stock transaction helps to resolve issues. It increases the daily trading volume, and if the new entity that will emerge from the merger decides to also list on the Toronto market, well, without taking anything from the Australian market, the Canadian market is already much more popular and trafficked than the Australian one, so that also makes trading much more attractive and interesting in terms of opportunities and their breadth.

Stock Prices: Bright Future Outlook From the Merger, but Volatility Offers a Possible Dip

At the time of writing, Mandalay Resources Corporation shares were trading under the symbol MNDJF on the U.S. OTCQB exchange at a price of $3.81 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $355.76 million.

Shares are trading very high in the cycle, trading very close to the upper end of the 52-week range of $1.45 to $4.14 per share. They are also above the MA Ribbon, and the RSI of 55.17 suggests that investors looking to strengthen their positions in order to have a larger stake in the merged company have ample downside room to bid significantly lower share prices.

Fed officials are concerned about the high level of uncertainty following more than two months of global trade disputes, as noted at the May FOMC meeting, with the risk of rising inflation remaining elevated for the upcoming months. The Fed is therefore being patient and waiting for more clarity on inflation and economic developments. The Fed maintained its target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25-4.50% for the third consecutive meeting in May 2025, but given the "wait and see" approach, headwinds for gold prices, a zero-yielding asset, will come from a higher-for-longer interest rates environment. This would pave the way for a decline in Mandalay Resources shares, consistent with the positive correlation between gold prices and Mandalay Resources' share price.

Conclusion

Mandalay Resources Corporation, a Canadian gold equivalent producer with facilities in Australia and Sweden, will merge with Australian miner Alkane Resources, which operates a gold mine in Australia and a copper project. Investing in Mandalay today offers the opportunity to acquire shares in a larger, more diversified, and value-creating company with growth potential and a more ample market presence. Potential investors may want to seek a significantly lower price than currently available.
