data.title: 2 Dividend Stocks I'd Marry If I Could
data.description:
Introduction

I think we can all agree that this year has been quite something.

  • We have been through one of the fastest bear markets ever.
  • This is one of the most headline-driven markets due to the tariff war.
  • On a short-term basis, we are somewhere between tariff-driven inflation and demand-weakness-related disinflation, which makes short-term macro predictions quite useless.
  • The dollar is under tremendous pressure, as bond yields are rising, further pressuring the government's plans to finance close to $7 trillion in debt this year. It also doesn't help that the U.S. is expected to maintain a very high deficit, with almost no chance of cutting spending without hurting growth (I think).

  • Related to the point above, international stocks have outperformed the S&P 500 quite substantially, as the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF (ACWX) is up 16% year-to-date, 'crushing' the S&P 500 by a wide margin.

  • Cyclical economic growth remains in a tough spot, as the main drivers of growth remain secular trends like the data center buildout, economic re-shoring, and programs related to government spending.

Needless to say, these are just a few points I came up with, as the bigger picture is way more complex than this. And to make matters worse, almost all of these factors are in some way related.

That's also why Liz Ann Sonders and Kevin Gordon of Charles Schwab presented such a diverse view in their 2025 Mid-Year Outlook, writing that:

One of the more difficult aspects of analyzing the market this year has been the significant shifts in sector leadership. Shown below is the fan favorite sector quilt; this version breaks performance down into rolling two-week periods. As you can see, there isn't one sector that has maintained the top position for more than two weeks. Perhaps more interesting is the fact that even though Energy was in first place for three of the two-week periods (the most of any sector), it is one of the worst-performing sectors year-to-date. Conversely, Industrials only held the top spot once, yet is leading year-to-date. - Charles Schwab

That's also why Schwab makes that case that "a ton of sector churn" will be driven by issues like tariff policymaking, which will make it incredibly hard to make sector recommendations, especially because of the ripple effect, which requires next-level thinking, as the winners and losers of the trade war may not even be that obvious on first sight.

We continue to expect a ton of sector churn, driven by the mercurial nature of tariff policymaking, which in turn will continue to make it difficult to make sector recommendations (we remain neutral on all 11 sectors). In fairness, there are sectors and industries that are at or near the epicenter of aggressive tariffs—such as import-dependent consumer businesses, for example. Yet, there are ripple effects associated with the potential for weaker spending and investment, which would ultimately hit a sector like Financials (which is looked at as a haven from the trade war these days). - Charles Schwab

To put it differently, stock picking has become a fascinating endeavour.

On the one hand, it is nearly impossible to pick stocks based on a short-term and mid-term thesis.

On the other hand, because of elevated volatility and unusual market behavior, we can buy fantastic companies if we apply the right long-term mindset. Stocks that may even benefit from volatility and/or offer attractive long-term value through attractive valuations and wide-moat business models.

Hence, based on everything we discussed so far, I'm using the second part of this article to present two of my all-time favorite dividend (growth) stocks that I would even own if I could own just 5 individual dividend stocks.

They perfectly fit my strategy, have wide-moat business models, the ability to beat the market, and everything it takes to consistently hike dividends.

CME Group (CME) - It's Hard To Come Up With A Better Idea In This Market

I have brought up CME Group on a semi-frequent basis over the past few years, and I'm not getting tired of it.

For starters, CME Group has the perfect business model for this market, as it is the world's largest operator of futures exchanges. Initially, it was formed through strategic mergers, including the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, the Chicago Board of Trade ("CBOT"), the New York Mercantile Exchange ("NYMEX"), and others.

As a result, almost all of its revenues come from transaction fees, with additional revenues from clearing and data services. It dominates areas like interest rates, agricultural commodities, energy, metals, and cryptocurrencies.

In addition to long-term secular growth from the ever-increasing demand for hedging products from corporations and increasing trading volumes, the company's business model allows it to benefit from elevated volatility, unlike almost any other company.

On top of benefiting from elevated volatility from the uncertainty I mentioned in the introduction, the company has grown its average daily volumes ("ADV") by 7% per year since 2015, boosting its net income by 12% per year.

Moreover, since 2020, it has grown its international ADVs by 10% per year, as it has shifted its focus to booming growth in international markets. Currently, more than 60% of its front office sales staff work outside the U.S.

On top of that, CME benefits from the fact that scaling its operations is super-efficient, as almost all trading is done online. Hence, products launched over the past five years created 2.4 million ADVs in 2024, boosted by micros (smaller futures), options, fixed income, energy commodities, and alternatives like crypto.

Furthermore, growth from elevated global volatility is expected to get support from data developments, as AI and other technologies allow retail investors to access data that used to be reserved for institutional clients only. CME expects this to support long-term growth. I agree with that, as AI is bullish for trading volumes instead of a disruptor of CME's business.

It has also shrugged off any concerns regarding new competition. While two years ago, the company started getting downgrades due to the new entrance of FMX, it has proven that its size gives it a clear edge in pricing and liquidity.

And, as a cherry on top, the company also has one of the S&P 500's best margin profiles, as it turned $6.1 billion in revenues last year into $3.7 billion of net income, a net margin of slightly more than 60%.

With regard to its dividend, the company, which has a rare AA- credit rating, usually distributes 100% of its free cash flow to shareholders. It does this through four regular quarterly dividends and one special dividend.

The quarterly dividend yields 1.8%. I expect its annualized dividend yield to be somewhere in the 4.0% to 4.5% range, as analysts expect free cash flow to rise to more than $4.4 billion by 2027. I am more bullish than that, purely based on my "higher for longer" thesis, which includes volatility.

That's also why I expect CME shares to return more than 13% per year going forward, as it trades at 25.3x earnings. While that may seem lofty, it's below its multi-decade average of 27.1x, and boosted by its impressive margin profile and mid-to-high single-digit annual EPS growth.

Because its margins are so high, and it returns almost everything to shareholders, investors apply a higher multiple, as earnings are "high quality."

In other words, to me, CME is the perfect stock for this market environment and one of my all-time favorite dividend stocks, in general.

Union Pacific Corp. (UNP) - A Cyclical Wide-Moat Gem Poised To Outperform

Union Pacific was one of the first five stocks I bought for my dividend portfolio. It's also a company I cannot imagine selling, ever.

While Union Pacific is a cyclical company, it has withstood the test of time, as it is one of just two Class I railroads that dominates the Western two-thirds of the United States, which includes servicing a network of 32,000 railroad miles across 23 states, connecting major ports and supporting almost every single supply chain you can think of.

On top of having limited competition, the U.S. would literally run into massive trouble if Union Pacific were to cease all operations overnight. While that will never happen, I like to use this as an example of how important Union Pacific is.

Moreover, under Jim Vena, who became CEO in 2023, the company has become the most efficient Class I railroad in all of North America, as it has improved its operations, boosted customer satisfaction, and lowered costs without sacrificing its ability to grow volumes.

While I may sound a bit dramatic, I believe Jim Vena and his team have shown the world a masterclass in railroading, with expectations of outperforming revenues, strong pricing dollars (above inflation), and further efficiency gains through at least 2027.

Moreover, as of June 10, when the company presented at the Wells Fargo Industrials & Materials Conference, it has seen an upswing in volumes, despite cyclical challenges.

Quarter-to-date, carloads are up 5%, driven by double-digit growth in bulk, a segment I am very bullish on, as it comes with strong secular growth in agriculture, chemicals, and support from intermodal due to economic re-shoring and truck-to-rail conversions.

On top of that, I am bullish on cyclical growth despite the elevated uncertainty we are dealing with right now. I elaborated on that in a recent article titled "Buried Under Fear: One Of The Best Opportunities I've Seen In Years."

It also needs to be said that despite cyclical headwinds, UNP always bounces back, as no recession in history has done lasting damage to the company.

In fact, the company, which has an A- credit rating, has a weighted average rate on its debt of just 3.8%, and a weighted average maturity of roughly 20 years, which is better than almost any company on my radar.

Thanks to this balance sheet, investors have consistently benefited from its success, as the company has hiked its dividend for 18 consecutive years, with a CAGR of 17%. During this period, it has also bought back 44% of its shares and boosted its return on invested capital to roughly 16%, an indication of excellence when it comes to investing in its own business.

Currently, UNP yields 2.4%, with a 47% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 6.7%.

I expect this number to accelerate, as analysts seem to agree with my thesis of stronger earnings growth.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect 4% EPS growth this year, potentially followed by 11% and 10% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

If we get a bottom in leading indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index, which marked the peak of UNP in 2021, we could see an annual return of at least 11-12% based on its 19.8x P/E ratio. I highlighted the 2021 peak of the ISM Manufacturing Index in the chart below.

For now, the market isn't expecting a growth acceleration, which is why EPS growth numbers mainly rely on the company's investments, buybacks, and secular tailwind.

In other words, while UNP is very different from CME, it remains one of my all-time favorite dividend stocks and a great company to buy when sentiment toward cyclical stocks is poor.

That's also why I expect the ratio of Union Pacific to the S&P 500 to bottom this year, which could lead to a return to the 2000-2021 period when Union Pacific consistently outperformed the market.

In the weeks ahead, I'll shed more light on high-quality cyclical dividend stocks, as I believe there are a lot of overlooked quality stocks that make tremendous investments. This includes lower-yielding growers and higher-yielding income stocks.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

This market is a puzzle. It's volatile, headline-driven, and full of contradictions, which makes it one of the toughest markets in many years.

As a result, short-term predictions are almost useless, which is just another reason why I feel my long-term dividend strategy is the best way to go.

In this article, I went a step further, as I discussed two of my all-time favorite dividend stocks, which I expect to deliver tremendous long-term value for a wide range of dividend investors.

  • CME thrives on uncertainty. As it's the world's largest futures exchange, it profits from volatility, while it also maintains super-high margins and a cash-rich business model. It also stands out because of its special dividend policy.

  • UNP is a cyclical powerhouse with a wide moat. Under Jim Vena's leadership, it has become the most efficient railroad in North America. Despite near-term economic growth fears, its volume rebound, pricing power, and 18-year dividend growth streak make it a cornerstone holding in all of my family's dividend portfolios.

Needless to say, all of this explains why we have consistently bought more of these stocks, using stock price weakness to our advantage.

Given my high conviction, that's the playbook I will continue to implement.

Risks To My Thesis

Besides general market and recession risks, I believe general risks to my thesis are limited, as CME's biggest risk is a prolonged period of subdued volatility and new competition.

Union Pacific's biggest risk is operational accidents, a failure to maintain high on-time rates, and the inability to benefit from growth drivers like economic re-shoring. Temporary risks are economic weakness and a loose trucking market, which could pressure intermodal transportation volumes and pricing.

data.title: Eurozone Industrial Production Unexpectedly Nosedives In April
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

Eurozone industrial production dropped sharply in April, partly wiping out the front-loading surge of the first quarter.

After an impressive first quarter, eurozone industrial production staged an expected nosedive in April, dropping by 2.4% month-on-month. On the year, it is still up by 0.8%. After the strong surge in the first quarter on the back of US front-loading of eurozone goods ahead of higher tariffs, industrial production showed the expected reversal. However, looking at the individual countries shows large divergence across the eurozone and once again a sharp distortion of eurozone data due to Ireland (which had a month-on-month drop by more than 15%). While Germany, France and Spain saw industrial production dropping, Italy recorded some improvement.

Industrial outlook between tariffs, rising oil prices and a turning inventory cycle

The eurozone manufacturing sector is currently highly affected by two main factors: the cyclical turning of the inventory cycle and Donald Trump’s tariffs. Tariffs and the related front-loading had been the main driver of surging industrial production in the first quarter. Lower energy prices as a result of the tariff announcements and erratic economic policy announcements in the US also seemed to improve the outlook for industry. It would seem that Israel’s military strikes against Iran last night have clearly erased hopes for sustainably low oil prices. With Brent trading again close to 80 USD/b, energy prices are back on the eurozone manufacturers’ worry list.

US tariffs and again highly elevated geopolitical risks provide reasons enough against any premature optimism. However, more fundamentally speaking, it looks like eurozone manufacturing has been bottoming out. The announced German fiscal stimulus, as well as European defense spending, have both added to order books and general optimism. But there's more. After huge stockpiling in 2021 and 2022 and high inventories limiting production since then, the inventory cycle is showing the first signs of turning. Even though it's not happening yet in all eurozone countries, it is most pronounced in Germany. Still, it is the combination of inventory reduction and improving order books that will boost production in the future.

So, while there are signs of optimism, the outlook for the eurozone's manufacturing sector remains incredibly uncertain. Just when things were starting to look more positive, geopolitical risks and those rising oil prices are once again casting a long shadow over recovery hopes.

data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Threat of war

Tensions are intensifying in the Middle East as Israel launched "pre-emptive" strikes on Iran's nuclear facilities and military sites, following which it declared a state of emergency in anticipation of retaliation from Tehran. Iran responded by launching over 100 drones at Israel overnight, heightening the risk of an all-out war in the region.

Conflict escalates: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu announced the launch of "Operation Rising Lion," which he said was a targeted military operation to "roll back the Iranian threat to Israel's very survival." Iran's supreme leader Ayatollah Khamenei warned of retaliation, saying Israel "has prepared for itself a bitter, painful fate." The International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed that Iran's Natanz nuclear facility was hit, but no increase in radiation levels was observed. As for Iran's drones, Israeli military spokesman Effie Deffrin said defense systems would intercept the threats.

U.S. stance: Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the U.S. was not involved in Israel's attack on Iran, adding that Israel informed Washington that the strikes were being carried out for self-defense. Prior to the attack, President Donald Trump had warned of a potential strike. To note, the U.S. had already pulled personnel from the Middle East earlier this week due to heightened regional tensions.

Markets react: While the world suspected Israel would launch an attack on Iran, the severity took markets by surprise, with oil prices surging 13% and U.S. stock futures down more than 1%. The U.S. and Iran were slated to continue nuclear talks this weekend, but now it's unclear if they'll go ahead. data.title: Israel's Strike Lifts Dollar But Only Modestly, Gold And Oil Rally
data.description:
Overview

Israel attacked Iranian nuclear enrichment site and apparently targeted scientists and top Revolutionary Guard leadership. Reports suggest that no increase in radioactivity has been detected. The US quickly indicated that while it was informed of the attack, it did not authorize it. President Trump did warn of a possible strike, but the press reports made it seem as if a strike was not imminent. The dollar is stronger against all the G10 currencies and most emerging market currencies; However, the gains have been limited, and some will question the dollar's safe haven status. Equities are heavier, with most European bourses off 1.0-1.5%, while US index futures are off the equivalent. Asia Pacific bourses were off by less.

Bond markets have not benefitted. European 10-year benchmark yields are mostly 2-4 bps higher. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is off a single basis point to near 4.35%. Gold, which the ECB estimated earlier this week to have replaced the euro as the second most important reserve asset after the dollar, is up a little more than 1% in late European morning turnover near $3386. July WTI is up about 7.5% near $69.

USD: Israel's strike on Iran has seen the Dollar Index recoup yesterday's losses that had seen it fall to a new three-year low in early North American turnover yesterday near 97.60. However, it has not been able to take out yesterday's high near 98.50, which will lead to talk about it losing its safe haven status, but strong conclusions will require some time. For release today, only the preliminary University of Michigan's consumer sentiment is due. A small improvement in confidence and a slight decline in inflation expectations are expected. In the tightening cycle, Fed Chair Powell pointed to the University of Michigan survey and sometimes, even the preliminary report; now, less so. The NY Fed's survey has not been as alarmist. Another way to get a handle on inflation expectations is derived from the market, such as the difference between yield on conventional instruments and the inflation-protected securities. The one-year breakeven, for example, is near 2.50%, the low for the year. The 5-10 year breakevens are a couple of basis points, around 2.30%.

Euro: The euro reached roughly $1.1630 in early North American dealing yesterday. It has not been this high since October 2021. It settled above it its upper Bollinger Band yesterday but was sold to about $1.1510 on Israel's strike and has held above yesterday's low near $1.1485. The eurozone industrial output fell sharply in April (-2.4%) and the trade surplus was halved to 14 bln euros (from a record surplus of nearly 29 bln euros in March). This is broadly consistent with the return to a slower growth trajectory after the 0.6% quarter-over-quarter expansion in Q1 25, which matched the strongest growth since Q2 22. However, the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey anticipates growth slowing to 0.1% in Q2 25 and Q3 25. The swaps market has slightly more than a 10% chance of a rate cut next month, but it rises to almost 60% for the September meeting, 75% for the October meeting, and it is nearly completely discounted before the end of the year.

CNY: The broadly weaker US dollar was threatening to end the yuan's consolidative phase. The greenback was turned down on Wednesday after briefly trading north of CNH7.20. It fell back toward CNH7.1715 yesterday. The broad but modest dollar gains lifted the greenback to almost CNH7.19 today. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate lower for the fourth consecutive session (CNY7.1772) and below CNY7.18 for the first time since April 1. China reported May lending figures that were in line with expectations. Early Monday, it will release May real sector data. The economy appears to continue to struggle to extend momentum.

JPY: The dollar was turned back on Wednesday after setting a new high for the month near JPY145.45. It reached almost JPY143.20 yesterday. The dollar initially fell to around JPY142.80 a new six-day low today on Israel's strike but recovered to almost JPY144 in the European morning. After becoming decoupled from US 10-year yield, the traditional driver, the rolling 30-day correlation is rising, and slightly above 0.40, it is at the highest in about two months. Last week, Japan's 0.7% annualized contraction in Q1 was revised to a more modest -0.2%. Still, Q2 has begun off poorly. Earlier today, Japan revised down April's 0.9% contraction in industrial output to -1.1% and the tertiary industry index (services) may eke out a modest 0.3% gain (after March's 0.3% decline was revised to -1.0%). The BOJ meets next week, but despite the firm inflation readings, seems to be in no hurry to raise rates. Indeed, the focus may not be on interest rate policy but on the BOJ's bond purchases. The BOJ has slowed its bond purchases, but this may have contributed to the volatility of the long end of the curve. It will reportedly consider making smaller reductions from the current pace of JPY400 bln (~$2.8 bln) a quarter. Some think the pace could be halved to JPY200 bln. At the end of March, the BOJ's balance sheet was about 118% of GDP, the smallest since May 2020. It peaked in 2022 near 133% of GDP.

GBP: Sterling set a new three-year high yesterday near $1.3625. The gains were despite weak jobs data and an unexpectedly large contraction in April's GDP, which boosted confidence of a rate cut in August (not next week). It made a marginal new high earlier today, slightly above $1.3630 before selling off to take out yesterday's low by about 1/100 of a cent (according to Bloomberg pricing). Sterling's weakness, however, is evident against the euro, where it has fallen to new lows since early May. The euro reached almost GBP0.8550, which corresponds to the (50%) retracement of the euro's losses since the April 11 high near GBP0.8740. It is consolidating today.

CAD: After reversing lower on Tuesday from around CAD1.3730, the greenback fell to CAD1.3600 yesterday, its lowest level since last October and dipped briefly below it in early trading before Israel's strike. It recovered to about CAD1.3650 before consolidating. Canada reports manufacturing and wholesale sales and capacity utilization figures. These do not capture the attention of the market, even in the best of times. Next week's highlights include April portfolio flows and retail sales. The swaps market has a little more than one cut discounted by the end of the year, which is currently seen as the end of the easing cycle.

AUD: The Australian dollar recovered from an eight-day low in early European turnover yesterday (almost $0.6475) to reach the $0.6535 area in the North American afternoon. Israel's military operation drove it to nearly $0.6455. It has subsequently recovered almost $0.6500 and is consolidating in the European morning above around $0.6480.

MXN: After falling to its lowest level since last August on Wednesday (~MXN18.9120), the greenback consolidated yesterday between approximately MXN18.8560 and MXN18.98. The peso is one of the only emerging market currencies that trades 24-hours a day. It is sometimes, therefore, used as a proxy for other emerging market currencies, especially in a risk-off event. The dollar spiked to around MXN19.08, but is hovering now near MXN19.00. In the recent past, such spikes were not sustained. Meanwhile, the above 4% inflation reported for May appears to have spurred some disagreement about the central bank's leadership. Deputy Governor Heath argued for a pause, while Governor Rodriguez continued to advocate for a 50 bps cut. The central bank meets on June 26. A compromise might be a 25 bp cut, and that is what the swaps market appears to be discounting as the most likely scenario.
